metric_name: Actual outcome: HP market share prediction (Will gain further share in 1996)
metric_value: HP maintained Unix server market leadership through 1996-1997. According to IDC, HP was the worldwide leader in technical Unix server shipments and revenue in 1996 and first half of 1998. HP PA-8000 64-bit systems shipped 1996-1998 maintained competitive edge. Prediction confirmed.
notes: Source: NetworkComputing HP PA-RISC article (2005) citing IDC data; OpenPA.net HP 9000 PA-RISC story. HP maintained #1 Unix market position as predicted.

metric_value: RISC/Unix market growth slowed significantly below predicted 40%+ in 1996-1997. NT Server shipments grew from 393K (1995) to 732K (1996) to 1.3M units (1997) vs Unix 717K units (IDC 1997). Overall server market grew only 10.8% in 1996-1997 versus 40%+ RISC/Unix growth in 1995. Linux x86 emergence accelerated RISC/Unix deceleration. Prediction not confirmed.
notes: Source: CNET 'Windows NT server market grows' (1998-01-27). The 40%+ growth continuation was wrong; market matured faster than Aberdeen forecast.

metric_name: Actual outcome: NT impact on RISC/Unix for business-critical apps
metric_value: NT Server captured departmental/workgroup segment (shipments tripling 1995-1997) but Unix held enterprise OLTP and mission-critical deployments through 2000. NT 4.0 shipped April 1996 as predicted. Unix held ~717K server units in 1997 with 15% modest growth. Prediction confirmed for 1996-1998 period: NT complemented, did not displace RISC/Unix for business-critical production apps.
notes: Source: CNET NT server market grows (1998-01-27): 'NT experienced most growth in departmental servers serving 20-50 people.' Prediction confirmed.

metric_name: Actual outcome: Year-2000 mainframe replacement cycle opportunity
metric_value: Y2K drove significant server infrastructure investment 1998-2000. IBM pSeries Unix revenue grew 28% in 2000, 49% in Q4 2000 (IBM Annual Report 2000). However much Y2K spending was split across RISC/Unix, Windows NT, and emerging Linux x86 rather than purely benefiting RISC/Unix. The opportunity materialized but was shared across platforms.
notes: Source: IBM Annual Report 2000 (ibm.com). Y2K opportunity confirmed but RISC/Unix shared it with NT/Linux. Prediction partially confirmed.

metric_name: Actual outcome: Production Unix functionality advances (1996 agenda item 1)
metric_value: All major RISC/Unix vendors delivered significant production Unix improvements in 1996-1998: HP-UX 10.20 (1996) with improved POSIX; AIX 4.2 (1996) with 64-bit support; Solaris 2.6/7 (1997/1998) with large files and 64-bit; Digital Unix 4.0 with enhanced clustering. POSIX Single Specification v2 (1997) consolidated Unix standards. Prediction confirmed.
notes: Source: ACM Queue 64-bit article (2006); Unix Single Specification history. Production functionality advances delivered by all major vendors as prescribed.

metric_name: Actual outcome: 64-bit Unix transition (1996 agenda item 2)
metric_value: DEC Alpha was already 64-bit (since 1992). HP shipped PA-8000 64-bit processors in 1996 for HP-UX. Sun shipped UltraSPARC-II with Solaris 7 64-bit in 1998. IBM AIX 4.2 (1996) added 64-bit addressing. All major RISC/Unix vendors (except IBM being already in AS/400 64-bit path) transitioned to 64-bit within 12-18 months as predicted.
notes: Source: ACM Queue 'Long Road to 64 Bits' (2006); OpenPA.net HP PA-RISC PA-8000 history. Prediction confirmed with minor timing variation.

metric_name: Actual outcome: Workgroup interoperability advances (1996 agenda item 3)
metric_value: Workgroup interoperability improved significantly 1996-1998: HP-UX Advanced Server for NT integration; IBM AIX and NT integration tools; Samba protocol (Unix-NT file sharing) matured rapidly; Novell NetWare for Unix expanded. NT integration became standard RISC/Unix feature by 1997. Prediction confirmed.
notes: Source: HP-UX NT integration documentation; Samba project history. NT/NetWare interoperability advances delivered as prescribed.

metric_name: Actual outcome: MPP platform maturation (1996 agenda item 4)
metric_value: MPP platforms matured significantly 1996-1998: IBM RS/6000 SP became IBM's Deep Blue platform (1997 chess victory); DEC TruCluster advanced; Oracle RAC emerged as software-based alternative to MPP hardware; Sequent NUMA-Q advanced. However proprietary MPP was ultimately displaced by commodity Linux clusters in 2000s. Prediction confirmed for 1996-1998 period.
notes: Source: IBM Annual Report 2000 (RS/6000 SP pSeries); RISC Disappointments PDF (1997). MPP maturation confirmed; eventual commodity Linux displacement came later.
